63	
64	# Blueprint Writer
65	
66	You write or revise **one blueprint chapter** under plan-agent direction. You receive a precise directive naming the target chapter, the strategy context the chapter must reflect, the definitions/theorems that must be present, and the scope of changes you may make.
67	
68	## Your Job
69	
70	The plan agent has decided that a specific blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`) needs to change — to reflect a strategic decision, fill a missing definition or theorem the project needs, or align the informal prose with the current Lean structure. Your directive tells you which chapter, what must be there, and what counts as out-of-scope.
71	
72	You only edit the **one chapter named in your directive**. Your declared `--write-domain` should reflect that (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`). You do NOT edit other chapters, `.lean` files, or `content.tex`. Cross-chapter inconsistencies you spot go in the report's "Notes for Plan Agent" section — you flag them, you don't fix them.
73	
74	## Directive Format
75	
76	```markdown
77	# Blueprint Writer Directive
78	
79	## Slug
80	<slug>
81	
82	## Target chapter
83	blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter-slug>.tex
84	
85	## Strategy context
86	<the relevant slice of STRATEGY.md the plan agent extracted for you — typically a paragraph or two describing what this chapter must support in the overall arc. You do NOT read STRATEGY.md yourself; the plan agent gives you only what is relevant.>
87	
88	## Required content
89	- Definition <name>: <informal description of what it must define, with the mathematical content the prover needs in order to formalize>
90	- Theorem <name>: <statement + intent of the proof sketch the prover should formalize>
91	- Proof sketch for <theorem>: <how detailed; what cross-references; whether to expand a step into sub-lemmas>
92	- ...
93	
94	## Out of scope
95	<things that look related but the plan agent does NOT want you to touch this round>
96	
97	## References
98	- <path/to/reference.md or arxiv ID>: <which sections are relevant>
99	- ...
100	
101	## Expected outcome
102	<what the chapter should look like after, in one paragraph>
103	```
104	
105	If a section is omitted from your directive, the plan agent decided you don't need it. Don't speculate beyond what was given.
106	
107	## Chapter format
108	
109	Each declaration block in a chapter looks like:
110	
111	```latex
112	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
113	  \label{thm:some_label}
114	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
115	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
116	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174  (read from references/hartshorne-III-5.md)
117	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A morphism $f: X \to Y$ of schemes locally of finite
118	  % type is said to be smooth at $x \in X$ if there exist an open affine
119	  % neighborhood $V = \Spec B$ of $f(x)$ and an open affine neighborhood
120	  % $U = \Spec A$ of $x$ with $f(U) \subset V$ such that ..."
121	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.5.1.}
122	  Informal statement of the theorem, in the project's notation.
123	\end{theorem}
124	
125	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Proof. We may assume $Y = \Spec B$ and
126	% $X = \Spec A$ are affine. Then $f$ corresponds to a ring homomorphism
127	% $\varphi: B \to A$, and $f$ is smooth at $x$ if and only if ..."
128	\begin{proof}
129	  \uses{thm:another_result}
130	  Step-by-step informal proof, in the project's notation. Reference blueprint labels
131	  with \uses{...} so the dependency graph stays accurate.
132	\end{proof}
133	```
134	
135	Use `\definition`, `\lemma`, `\theorem`, `\proposition`, `\corollary` as appropriate. `\lean{...}` names the Lean declaration this block corresponds to. `\uses{...}` records cross-references; keep it accurate so `leanblueprint`'s dependency graph remains usable.
136	
137	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
138	
139	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
140	
141	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
142	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
143	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.
144	   - **Original notation preserved.** If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ and the project uses $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the verbatim quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The project-notation restatement happens in the prose body that follows, NOT in the quote.
145	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
146	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
147	
148	**For proof blocks**: include a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). Same verbatim rules — original language, original notation, every word. The informal proof body inside `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}` is the project's restated version in project notation, what the prover formalizes.
149	
150	When a source proof is too long for one verbatim block (multi-page construction): split the theorem into sub-lemmas in the directive's logical structure, and give each sub-statement its own `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`. One opaque mega-quote defeats verifiability. If even sub-splitting is impractical, report it in "Notes for Plan Agent" — do not silently drop the verbatim quote.
151	
152	**For Archon-original / project-bespoke results** (the directive does not name an external source for this block): the source lines are omitted — the block stands on the proof sketch alone.
153	
154	**The hard rule, explicit:**
155	
156	> **You may NEVER write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from training memory.** Every such line must be backed by a local file under `references/` that you have **opened and read in this session**, and the verbatim quote must be a character-by-character copy of text from that file.
157	
158	The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical in `% SOURCE:` is your discipline check. If, when reviewing your own draft, you find a block where you cannot point to the specific local file you read to produce the quote — you fabricated it. Delete the quote. Either dispatch a `reference-retriever` (see below) to obtain the missing source, or report the block as INCOMPLETE under "Notes for Plan Agent".
159	
160	If the directive named a source but the local reference file is missing or doesn't contain the specific statement you need:
161	
162	- **Dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** (see "Dispatching a reference-retriever" below) to fetch the missing material into `references/<slug>.md`.
163	- **Wait for the retriever to return**, then **open and read the new file**.
164	- THEN write the citation block, citing the new local file in the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
165	
166	If retrieval fails (paywall, broken link, no API key, not available online): mark the block `% SOURCE: <pointer> (verbatim text not yet retrieved)` and skip it. Report it as INCOMPLETE. Do NOT substitute a paraphrase, a recollection, or a translation as the verbatim quote — that's the iter-149 failure mode and the entire rule exists to prevent it.
167	
168	## Rules
169	
170	### What you CAN do
171	- Add new declaration blocks (definitions, lemmas, theorems, propositions, corollaries) under direction.
172	- Expand or revise existing prose / proof sketches in your assigned chapter.
173	- **Add, correct, or complete `\uses{...}` cross-references** so the dependency edges match the real mathematics. If your chapter's proof of `T` invokes lemma `L`, `T` must declare `\uses{lem:L}`; if a `\uses{}` points at a label that no longer exists, fix it. Verify with `leandag` (see "Verifying dependencies with leandag" below) — do not eyeball it.
174	- Adjust `\lean{...}` hints when the directive names a new Lean target.
175	- **Write entries for Lean helper declarations.** The project keeps a 1-to-1 Lean ↔ blueprint correspondence: when the directive lists a helper that exists only in Lean (a prover-created lemma, an instance, a decomposition step), give it a real entry — concise informal statement, `\label{}`, `\lean{}` with the exact Lean name, accurate `\uses{}`, and at least a one-line informal proof. "It's trivial" is not a reason to skip it: the entry is what carries the dependency edges and what helps a later prover fill the sorry. When the helper's Lean proof relies on a fact with no blueprint entry, flag it in "Notes for Plan Agent" (or create it too, if it belongs in your chapter).
176	- **Author a *Mathlib dependency anchor* and mark it `\mathlibok`** when your chapter relies on a result Mathlib already provides — write out the statement and point `\lean{}` at the real Mathlib declaration (see "Mathlib dependency anchors" below). This is the only marker you may add.
177	- **Remove a declaration block ONLY when the directive explicitly lists it for removal** (typically an isolated/orphaned statement the reviewer flagged `remove`). Default to wiring an isolated node into the graph (add the missing `\uses{}`), not deleting it — an isolated node is usually a missing edge, not dead weight. Never remove the project goal, and never remove a block whose `\lean{}` still names a live Lean declaration.
178	- Read `references/summary.md` and any reference that is in `references/` to ground your writing in the project's sources.
179

181	- **Keep the chapter valid LaTeX.** Don't leave dangling `\begin{...}` without matching `\end{...}`. Compile-checking is the plan agent's responsibility but you must not introduce syntax errors.
182	- **Stay within your chapter.** Your declared write-domain is one `*.tex` file. The Archon CLI rejects writes outside it.
183	- **Define non-standard macros in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex`** before using them — but: that file is outside your write-domain, so you DON'T touch it. If the directive requires a new macro, you note in your report "needs macro `\foo`" and leave the LaTeX using the new command name; the plan agent adds the macro before next iter's typeset.
184	- **Use mathematical, not Lean-syntactic prose.** Describe the proof in the language of mathematics — definitions, set inclusions, ring maps, universal properties — not in Lean tactic syntax. The prover formalizes your math.
185	- **Follow citation discipline** for every block derived from external reference material (see "Citation discipline (the hard rule)" above). Each such block needs `% SOURCE:` with a local-file parenthetical, `% SOURCE QUOTE:` with a verbatim original-language quote, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` before the proof env when applicable, and a visible `\textit{Source: ...}` prefix. Never cite from memory — only from a local `references/<file>.md` you opened and read in this session.
186	- **Document every change** in your report, including which `references/<file>.md` files you opened (under "References consulted").
187	
188	### What you MUST NOT do
189	- **Do NOT reference project history.** No "since iteration N," "after our failed attempt," or session narrative. The blueprint must read as a standalone mathematical document.
190	- **Do NOT include Lean implementation details.** No tactic suggestions, typeclass wiring notes, or Lean code structure hints in the prose. If a Lean note is needed, it belongs in `.lean` files (the plan agent will use `lean-scaffolder` for that).
191	- **Do NOT add `\leanok` markers.** `\leanok` is earned by a sorry-free Lean proof and set by the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase — never by you. (`\mathlibok` is different: you MAY add it, but ONLY on a Mathlib dependency anchor — see "Mathlib dependency anchors" below — never on the project's own to-be-proved declarations.)
192	- **Do NOT edit other chapters.** Even when you spot a related issue, flag it in "Notes for Plan Agent" instead of fixing it.
193	- **Do NOT edit `content.tex`** (the top-level blueprint file that `\input`s the chapters).
194	- **Do NOT edit `.lean` files** or any other state file.
195	- **Do NOT write Lean syntax** — keep the chapter mathematical, not syntactic.
196	- **Do NOT write literal `REF` placeholders.** Never produce prose like "Definition~REF" or "Sections REF–REF". Cross-reference declarations with `\cref{<label>}` using the real label (look it up in the chapter or via `leandag`); when the target genuinely has no label yet, describe it by name in prose. The blueprint-doctor flags every literal `REF` as a malformed reference.
197	- **Do NOT interleave math delimiters.** Pick `\( … \)` for a formula and close it with `\)` — never produce `$ … \( … \) … $` or `X\(, hence \)Y` inversions; the doctor's `math-delim` lint flags every such site and the rendered output shreds mid-formula.
198	- **Do NOT paste bare label ids into prose.** "Thm.~th:main" is not a reference — write `\cref{...}` for project declarations, or the source's human-readable number ("Thm.~4.8") when quoting external material.
199	- **Do NOT use undefined macros.** Every `\foo` your prose needs must exist in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex` or be `\providecommand`'d at the top of your chapter (chapter-local is fine; report a "needs macro" note for shared ones).
200	- **Do NOT touch protected blocks.** Your invocation prompt's "Protected by the mathematician" section lists `archon-protected.yaml` rules. A `statement`-protected label's declaration block (statement + `\label`/`\lean`/`\uses`) is frozen — you may still write its `\begin{proof}`; an `all`-protected label is entirely off-limits. You may freely `\uses{}` protected labels from other blocks. If your directive conflicts with a protection rule, stop and report the conflict instead of editing.
201	- **Do NOT expand scope.** Stick to what the directive listed under "Required content".
202	- **Do NOT fabricate citations.** Never write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from memory. If you don't have a local `references/<file>.md` containing the source text, dispatch a retriever and wait. The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical is the discipline check — if you cannot truthfully point to the file you read, the citation block is not allowed in the chapter.
203	- **Do NOT translate or restate the verbatim quote.** `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` contain the source's original language, original notation, every word as-is. Project-notation rewrites belong in the rendered prose body, not in the verbatim comment.
204	
205	## Mathlib dependency anchors (`\mathlibok`)
206	
207	When your chapter's proofs rely on a definition or lemma that **Mathlib already provides** (and the project will not re-prove), it is good practice — not just a bare `\uses{}` to nowhere — to make that dependency a first-class blueprint block: a **Mathlib dependency anchor**. Doing so:
208	
209	- makes the reliance explicit and readable (a mathematician sees exactly which Mathlib result the route stands on);
210	- gives `leandag` a real node, so the `\uses{}` resolves (no broken/unknown ref) and the node's effort is **done** rather than ∞;
211	- keeps it clear how much the route leans on Mathlib vs. on original work.
212	
213	Write the anchor like any statement block, with three differences:
214	
215	```latex
216	\begin{lemma}[Hensel's lemma]
217	  \label{lem:hensel_mathlib}
218	  \lean{Mathlib.RingTheory.Henselian.foo}   % the REAL Mathlib declaration
219	  \mathlibok
220	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
221	  Statement of the result in the project's notation.
222	\end{lemma}
223	```
224	
225	- **`\lean{}` names the actual Mathlib declaration** (with its real namespace), so `leandag` matches the node to Mathlib.
226	- **`\mathlibok`** marks it as supplied by Mathlib — the DAG treats it as done; no `\begin{proof}` is needed.
227	- No `% SOURCE:`/`% SOURCE QUOTE:` block — Mathlib is the source; the `\lean{}` target *is* the citation.
228	
229	**The anti-hallucination rule (mirrors citation discipline):** mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib and your stated form is faithful to it. A wrong or invented `\mathlibok` is worse than an ∞ hole — the loop will treat a non-existent result as available and skip proving a real gap. So:
230	
231	- Prefer anchors the **directive names** (the plan agent/reviewer identified them as Mathlib results).
232	- If you are not confident the exact statement is in Mathlib, do **not** mark `\mathlibok` — write the block without it (or leave the `\uses{}` for the reviewer to resolve) and flag it under "Notes for Plan Agent" so the reviewer can verify against Lean.
233	- Never put `\mathlibok` on one of the project's **own** to-be-proved declarations. That marker is for Mathlib-supplied results only; `\leanok` (project proofs) is never yours to add.
234	
235	## Reading references
236	
237	You write mathematics, not literature criticism — your prose must be grounded in authoritative sources, not your training memory. Before composing any new declaration block:
238	
239	1. **Read `references/summary.md`** to see the index of sources the project already has. Sources directly relevant to your chapter are your first stop.
240	2. **Read every reference file your directive names** under its `## References` section. The plan agent named those because the chapter needs them.
241	3. **Read anything in `references/summary.md` your directive didn't name but that is clearly relevant** to your chapter (same area, same theorem, same construction).
242	
243	Do NOT write content from your training memory when you could ground it in a reference instead. When the references say one thing and your memory says another, trust the references.
244	
245	## Dispatching a reference-retriever (when the project's sources don't have what you need)
246	
247	If, while drafting, you discover that the chapter needs material the project's `references/` doesn't contain, **dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** rather than guessing or papering over the gap.
248	
249	Conditions for dispatch:
250	
251	- The directive named a source (`Smith 2018`, an arXiv ID) that isn't in `references/` yet.
252	- The required content is in a known textbook (Hartshorne, Vakil, Stacks Project, etc.) but the project hasn't summarized the relevant chapter.
253	- You need a Mathlib-adjacent source (nLab, math overflow, Stacks Project) the directive didn't anticipate.
254	
255	Dispatch (Bash; treat as blocking and await the report; in your write-domain only if it includes `references/**`):
256	
257	```
258	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
259	  --name reference-retriever \
260	  --slug <kebab-slug-for-the-source> \
261	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<your-slug>/reference-retriever-<child-slug>-directive.md \
262	  --write-domain 'references/**'
263	```
264	
265	Write the directive file first; the directive format is documented in `.archon/subagents/reference-retriever.md`. The retriever returns when the new summary is on disk; **then resume writing**, citing the new reference.
266	
267	If your invocation's recorded write-domain does NOT include `references/**` (your `parent.write_domain` in `dispatch.jsonl`), the dispatch will be rejected. In that case, STOP writing the affected section, report the missing source in "Notes for Plan Agent", and finish the parts of the chapter you can still write. The plan agent will re-dispatch you next iter with the broader write-domain.
268	
269	## Workflow

1	\chapter{The tautological quotient and the universal property of $\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)$}
2	\label{chap:Picard_GrassmannianQuot}
3	
4	\providecommand{\ShMod}{\mathrm{SheafOfModules}}
5	
6	The previous chapter constructed the Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over
7	\(\mathbb{Z}\) by gluing the affine charts \(U^I\)
8	(\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) along the transition isomorphisms
9	(\cref{def:gr_transition}) satisfying the cocycle condition
10	(\cref{lem:gr_cocycle}), and proved that the structure morphism
11	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is separated (\cref{lem:gr_separated})
12	and proper (\cref{lem:gr_proper}). This chapter adds the next layer: the
13	\emph{tautological rank-\(d\) quotient}
14	\(u : \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) of the
15	trivial rank-\(r\) sheaf, and the \emph{universal property} that makes
16	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) the fine moduli space of rank-\(d\) locally free quotients of
17	\(\mathcal{O}^{r}\) -- equivalently, the representing scheme of the Quot functor
18	of the trivial sheaf.
19	
20	Throughout, \(r \ge d \ge 1\) are the fixed integers of the previous chapter, and
21	for a size-\(d\) subset \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) we write \(R^I\) for the
22	chart ring \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) of \(U^I = \Spec R^I\) and \(R^I_J\) for the
23	localisation along \(P^I_J = \det(X^I_J)\) defining the overlap
24	\(U^I_J \subseteq U^I\).
25	
26	\section{Infrastructure: locally free sheaves and module gluing}
27	\label{sec:grquot_infra}
28	
29	The constructions of this chapter -- the universal quotient sheaf
30	\(\mathcal{U}\), the tautological quotient \(u\), and the functor of points -- rest
31	on two pieces of foundational machinery for sheaves of modules. The first is the
32	predicate ``locally free of rank \(d\)'' (\cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}),
33	which records that a sheaf of modules is, on the members of some open cover,
34	isomorphic to the trivial bundle \(\mathcal{O}^{d}\); it is the condition cut out
35	in the definition of the Grassmannian functor. That predicate is the project-local
36	\(\mathrm{SheafOfModules.IsLocallyFreeOfRank}\) already set up in the Quot-scheme
37	chapter (\cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}), which this chapter reuses verbatim;
38	the only foundational machinery genuinely new to this chapter is the second piece:
39	a gluing primitive that descends a sheaf of modules (and a morphism of such
40	sheaves) from per-chart data along an open cover, given a transition cocycle
41	compatible with the cover's own gluing; it is the vehicle that assembles the
42	per-chart free sheaves \(\mathcal{O}^{d}_{U^I}\) and the per-chart quotients
43	\(u^I\) into the global \(\mathcal{U}\) and \(u\), and is set up below.
44	
45	\begin{definition}
46	
47	\leanok
48	[Gluing a sheaf of modules along an open cover]
49	  \label{def:scheme_modules_glue}
50	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glue}
51	  \uses{def:gr_the_glue_data}
52	  % NOTE: (iter-051) The Lean signature currently carries only hypothesis (C1)
53	  % (self-identity, the `_hC1` argument); (C2) triple-overlap multiplicativity is
54	  % NOT yet a parameter and the body is `sorry`. Adding (C2) requires a module-level
55	  % pullback base-change transport along the glue datum's `t_fac`/`t'`, which is not
56	  % yet built (a naive eqToHom formulation is ill-typed → whnf runaway). (C2) must be
57	  % added to the signature before the `sorry` body can close. See task_results
58	  % AlgebraicJacobian_Picard_GrassmannianQuot.md (iter-051).
59	  Let \(D\) be a glue datum of schemes (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}), with index set \(J\), charts \(U_i\),
60	  pairwise overlaps \(V_{ij}\), open immersions
61	  \(f_{ij} : V_{ij} \hookrightarrow U_i\), and transition isomorphisms
62	  \(t_{ij} : V_{ij} \xrightarrow{\sim} V_{ji}\) satisfying the glue datum's
63	  cocycle conditions; write \(X\) for the glued scheme and
64	  \(\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\) for the canonical open immersions of the
65	  charts. Suppose given:
66	  \begin{itemize}
67	    \item for each \(i \in J\), a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\)-modules \(\mathcal{M}_i\) on the chart \(U_i\);
68	    \item for each pair \((i,j)\), a \emph{transition isomorphism} of sheaves of
69	      modules on the overlap \(V_{ij}\),
70	      \[
71	        g_{ij} \;:\; f_{ij}^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_i
72	          \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
73	        (t_{ij} \circ f_{ji})^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_j,
74	      \]
75	      comparing the two charts' sheaves after restriction (pullback) to the
76	      common overlap \(V_{ij}\);
77	  \end{itemize}
78	  \emph{subject to the following module cocycle conditions}, compatible with the
79	  scheme-level cocycle of \(D\) (its triple transition data \(t_{ij}\) and the
80	  overlap immersions \(f_{ij}\)), which are required as hypotheses on the family
81	  \((g_{ij})\):
82	  \begin{enumerate}
83	    \item[(C1)] \emph{self-identity:} the self-transition is the identity,
84	      \(g_{ii} = \mathrm{id}\) on \(f_{ii}^{*}\mathcal{M}_i\) (with
85	      \(V_{ii}\) and \(t_{ii}\) the diagonal data of \(D\));
86	    \item[(C2)] \emph{triple-overlap multiplicativity:} over each triple overlap
87	      \(V_{ij} \cap V_{ik}\) one has
88	      \(g_{jk} \circ g_{ij} = g_{ik}\), the two sides being compared after
89	      transport through the scheme-level triple transition of \(D\)
90	      (\(t_{ij}\) and the overlap immersions \(f_{ij}, f_{jk}, f_{ik}\)) so that
91	      all three pullbacks land on the same sheaf -- the multiplicative,
92	      \(\mathrm{GL}_d\)-valued cocycle condition.
93	  \end{enumerate}
94	  Conditions (C1) and (C2) are exactly the descent datum for the Zariski cover
95	  \(\{\iota_i\}\); it is subject to them that the gluing below is well-defined and
96	  the glued sheaf is characterised up to unique isomorphism. Out of this data the
97	  construction produces:
98	  \begin{enumerate}
99	    \item a glued sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{M}\) on the glued scheme \(X\); and
100	    \item for each \(i \in J\) a \emph{restriction isomorphism}
101	      \(\rho_i : \iota_i^{*}\,\mathcal{M} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{M}_i\)
102	      identifying the pullback of \(\mathcal{M}\) along \(\iota_i\) with the
103	      \(i\)-th chart sheaf, the \(\rho_i\) being compatible with the transition
104	      isomorphisms \(g_{ij}\) over the overlaps.
105	  \end{enumerate}
106	  The pair \((\mathcal{M}, (\rho_i)_i)\) is characterised up to unique
107	  isomorphism by the restriction property: \(\mathcal{M}\) is the unique sheaf of
108	  modules on \(X\) (up to canonical isomorphism) restricting to each
109	  \(\mathcal{M}_i\) compatibly with \((g_{ij})\). The same primitive descends
110	  morphisms: given two such glued data \((\mathcal{M}_i, g_{ij})\) and
111	  \((\mathcal{N}_i, h_{ij})\) with glued sheaves \(\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}\), and
112	  a family of \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\)-linear morphisms
113	  \(\phi_i : \mathcal{M}_i \to \mathcal{N}_i\) that commute with the transition
114	  isomorphisms on every overlap, there is a unique morphism
115	  \(\phi : \mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{N}\) of sheaves of modules on \(X\) with
116	  \(\rho_i^{\mathcal{N}} \circ \iota_i^{*}\phi = \phi_i \circ \rho_i^{\mathcal{M}}\)
117	  for every \(i\) -- the descent of \(\mathrm{Hom}\) along the cover.
118	
119	  \emph{Construction.} A sheaf of modules on \(X\) is equivalent to a compatible
120	  family of sheaves on the members of an open cover of \(X\) together with
121	  identifications on the overlaps -- effective descent for the Zariski cover
122	  \(\{\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\}\). The charts \(U_i\), being the images of
123	  the glue datum's objects, form such a cover, with intersections
124	  \(U_i \cap U_j\) identified with the overlaps \(V_{ij}\) via the structural data
125	  of \(D\). Pulling the sections of an abstract \(\mathcal{M}\) back to each chart
126	  must reproduce \(\mathcal{M}_i\); the transition isomorphisms \(g_{ij}\),
127	  satisfying the cocycle conditions, are exactly the descent datum that makes the
128	  family \((\mathcal{M}_i)\) glue, so the descent produces \(\mathcal{M}\) with
129	  the asserted restriction isomorphisms \(\rho_i\). The morphism statement is the
130	  descent of morphisms for the same cover: a morphism on \(X\) is a family of
131	  morphisms on the charts agreeing on overlaps, and the compatibility of the
132	  \(\phi_i\) with \((g_{ij}, h_{ij})\) is precisely the agreement condition,
133	  giving the unique glued \(\phi\). This specialises the general descent of sheaves
134	  of modules to the concrete open cover supplied by a scheme glue datum,
135	  and is the form in which the Grassmannian's tautological bundle and quotient are
136	  built.
137	
138	  \emph{Realisation (construction path).} Concretely, instantiate \(D\) as the
139	  Grassmannian glue datum (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}), with glued scheme
140	  \(X = \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) and its canonical open cover by the charts
141	  \(\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\). The glued sheaf \(\mathcal{M}\) is built from
142	  the per-chart sheaves \(\mathcal{M}_i\) and the overlap transition isomorphisms
143	  \(g_{ij}\) satisfying the module cocycle conditions, and is pinned down by the
144	  restriction isomorphisms \(\rho_i : \iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M} \xrightarrow{\sim}
145	  \mathcal{M}_i\) compatible with the \(g_{ij}\). For an open immersion
146	  \(\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\), the pullback \(\iota_i^{*}\) restricts sheaves
147	  of modules on \(X\) to sheaves of modules on \(U_i\). The transition isomorphisms
148	  \(g_{ij}\), forming a cocycle for this cover, constitute a descent datum; gluing the
149	  local sections yields \(\mathcal{M}\) together with the restriction isomorphisms
150	  \(\rho_i\), where both the assembly and the characterisation up to unique isomorphism
151	  rest on the locality of sections of a sheaf -- a compatible family of sections over
152	  the cover, agreeing on the overlaps, extends to a unique global section.
153	
154	  The morphism-descent half is the same locality argument applied to homomorphisms:
155	  a family \(\phi_i : \mathcal{M}_i \to \mathcal{N}_i\) commuting with the transition
156	  isomorphisms over every overlap is a compatible family of local morphisms, and the
157	  uniqueness of gluing of sections promotes it to a unique global
158	  \(\phi : \mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{N}\) of sheaves of modules on \(X\) restricting
159	  to each \(\phi_i\) through the \(\rho_i\).
160	\end{definition}
161	
162	\section{The tautological quotient on the charts}
163	\label{sec:grquot_chart}
164	
165	\emph{Infrastructure: scalar endomorphisms.} Realising a matrix of regular
166	functions as a morphism of free sheaves of modules has no off-the-shelf primitive
167	in Mathlib; it is assembled from two small project-local helpers that turn a global
168	function into a scalar endomorphism of the structure sheaf, after which the matrix
169	is reconstituted entry-by-entry over a finite biproduct.
170	
171	\begin{definition}\leanok
172	[Global section of the unit module]
173	  \label{def:gr_globalUnitSection}
174	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.globalUnitSection}
175	  \uses{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
176	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(a \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) a global section of
177	  the structure sheaf. Viewing \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as the unit module
178	  \(\mathbf{1} \in \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}), the
179	  section \(a\) determines a global section of \(\mathbf{1}\): on each open
180	  \(V \subseteq X\) take the restriction \(a|_V \in \Gamma(V, \mathcal{O}_X)\), the
181	  family \((a|_V)_V\) being compatible with the restriction maps because
182	  restriction is functorial. This is the section of the unit sheaf attached to a
183	  global function.
184	\end{definition}
185	
186	\begin{definition}\leanok
187	[Scalar endomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)]
188	  \label{def:gr_scalarEnd}
189	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scalarEnd}
190	  \uses{def:gr_globalUnitSection, lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
191	  For \(a \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) the \emph{scalar endomorphism}
192	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(a) : \mathcal{O}_X \to \mathcal{O}_X\) is the
193	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear endomorphism of the unit module given by multiplication
194	  by \(a\). It is obtained from the global section of \cref{def:gr_globalUnitSection}
195	  under the canonical identification of endomorphisms of the unit module
196	  \(\mathbf{1}\) with its global sections, \(\End(\mathbf{1}) \cong
197	  \Gamma(X, \mathbf{1})\); concretely, on each open \(V\) it acts as
198	  multiplication by \(a|_V\). These scalar endomorphisms are the matrix-entry
199	  building blocks of the chart quotient.
200	\end{definition}
201	
202	\begin{definition}
203	
204	\leanok
205	[Chart quotient \(u^I\)]
206	  \label{def:gr_chart_quotient}
207	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartQuotientMap}
208	  \uses{def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_scalarEnd}
209	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
210	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
211	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L898-L910).
212	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
213	  % "We next define a rank $d$ locally free sheaf $\U$ on $\grass(r,d)$
214	  %  together with a surjective homomorphism $\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$.
215	  %  On each $U^I$ we define a surjective homomorphism
216	  %  $u^I :\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I} \to  \oplus^d\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I}$
217	  %  by the matrix $X^I$."
218	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
219	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). On the affine chart
220	  \(U^I = \Spec R^I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) define the
221	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}\)-linear homomorphism of trivial bundles
222	  \[
223	    u^I \;:\; \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}
224	      \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}
225	  \]
226	  given by left multiplication by the universal \(d \times r\) matrix \(X^I\)
227	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}): on global sections it is the \(R^I\)-linear
228	  map \((R^I)^{r} \to (R^I)^{d}\), \(v \mapsto X^I v\). Since the \(I\)-th minor
229	  \(X^I_I\) of \(X^I\) is the identity matrix \(1_{d \times d}\), the composite of
230	  \(u^I\) with the inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates
231	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d} \hookrightarrow \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\) is the
232	  identity; hence \(u^I\) is a split surjection of locally free sheaves, with
233	  target the free rank-\(d\) sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\).
234	
235	  \emph{Realisation.} The free sheaves \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\) and
236	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\) are the finite biproducts (coproducts) of copies of
237	  the unit module \(\mathbf{1} = \mathcal{O}_{U^I}\), indexed by \(\{1,\dots,r\}\)
238	  and \(\{1,\dots,d\}\). Each entry \(X^I_{p,q} \in R^I\) of the universal matrix
239	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}) is a global function on \(U^I = \Spec R^I\),
240	  obtained from \(R^I\) through the canonical identification
241	  \(\Gamma(\Spec R^I, \mathcal{O}) \cong R^I\); the corresponding scalar
242	  endomorphism \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(X^I_{p,q}) : \mathbf{1} \to \mathbf{1}\)
243	  (\cref{def:gr_scalarEnd}) is the \((p,q)\)-component of \(u^I\). Assembling these
244	  components over the index biproducts -- the universal property of the biproduct
245	  packaging a matrix of component morphisms into a single morphism of the free
246	  sheaves -- yields \(u^I\). This entry-by-entry assembly via the finite biproduct
247	  is the substitute for a (non-existent) Mathlib ``matrix \(\mapsto\) morphism of
248	  free sheaves'' primitive.
249	\end{definition}
250	
251	\begin{lemma}\leanok
252	[The chart quotient is an epimorphism]
253	  \label{lem:gr_chartQuotientMap_epi}
254	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartQuotientMap_epi}
255	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient}
256	  The chart quotient \(u^I : \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\)
257	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}) is an epimorphism of sheaves of modules.
258	\end{lemma}
259	
260	\begin{proof}
261	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient}
262	  We exhibit a section, so that \(u^I\) is a \emph{split} epimorphism and hence an
263	  epimorphism. Let \(\iota_I : \{1,\dots,d\} \hookrightarrow \{1,\dots,r\}\) be the
264	  inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates, \(k \mapsto \iota_I(k)\), the
265	  \(k\)-th element of \(I\) in increasing order. It induces a morphism of free
266	  sheaves \(s_I : \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\), the
267	  coordinate inclusion sending the \(k\)-th basis section to the
268	  \(\iota_I(k)\)-th. We claim
269	  \[
270	    u^I \circ s_I \;=\; \mathrm{id}_{\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}}.
271	  \]
272	  Both sides are morphisms out of the free rank-\(d\) sheaf, which is the coproduct
273	  of \(d\) copies of the unit module; by the universal property of that coproduct
274	  it suffices to check the equality on each coordinate, i.e.\ to compare component
275	  matrices. The \((p,k)\)-component of \(u^I \circ s_I\) is the entry
276	  \(X^I_{p,\iota_I(k)}\) of the universal matrix at the \(I\)-indexed column, which
277	  is precisely the \(I\)-th minor \(X^I_I\) of \(X^I\). Since that minor is the
278	  \(d \times d\) identity (the columns of \(X^I\) indexed by \(I\), read through the
279	  order isomorphism \(\{1,\dots,d\} \cong I\), form the identity block --
280	  \cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}), one has \(X^I_{p,\iota_I(k)} = \delta_{p,k}\),
281	  so \(u^I \circ s_I\) has the identity component matrix and equals
282	  \(\mathrm{id}\). Hence \(s_I\) splits \(u^I\) and \(u^I\) is a split
283	  epimorphism, in particular an epimorphism.
284	\end{proof}
285	
286	\begin{definition}
287	
288	\leanok
289	[The universal quotient sheaf \(\mathcal{U}\)]
290	  \label{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}
291	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalQuotient}
292	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_universalMinorInv, def:gr_transition,
293	    lem:gr_cocycle, def:gr_glued_scheme, def:scheme_modules_glue,
294	    def:is_locally_free_of_rank}
295	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
296	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
297	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L903-L910).
298	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
299	  % "Compatible with the co-cycle $(\theta_{I,J})$
300	  %  for gluing the affine pieces $U^I$, we give gluing data
301	  %  $(g_{I,J})$ for gluing together the trivial bundles $\oplus^d\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I}$
302	  %  by putting
303	  %  $$g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1} \in GL_d(U^I_J)$$"
304	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
305	  Over each overlap \(U^I_J = \Spec R^I_J\) define the bundle-transition matrix
306	  \[
307	    g_{I,J} \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} \;\in\; \mathrm{GL}_d(R^I_J),
308	  \]
309	  the Cramer inverse of the localised \(J\)-minor (\cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}),
310	  which is invertible because \(\det(X^I_J)\) is a unit of \(R^I_J\). The matrices
311	  \((g_{I,J})\) are compatible with the chart-gluing cocycle \((\theta_{I,J})\)
312	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{lem:gr_cocycle}): over a triple overlap they
313	  satisfy \(g_{I,K} = g_{J,K}\, g_{I,J}\) and \(g_{I,I} = 1\), the multiplicative
314	  cocycle condition for a vector bundle. Gluing the free rank-\(d\) sheaves
315	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\) along the isomorphisms \(g_{I,J}\) over the cover
316	  \(\{U^I\}\) of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) therefore
317	  produces a locally free \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}\)-module
318	  \(\mathcal{U}\) of rank \(d\). Its determinant line bundle \(\det \mathcal{U}\)
319	  has transition functions \(\det(g_{I,J}) = 1/P^I_J\), the data underlying the
320	  Pl\"ucker embedding.
321	\end{definition}
322	
323	\begin{definition}
324	
325	\leanok
326	[The tautological quotient \(u : \mathcal{O}^{r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\)]
327	  \label{def:tautological_quotient}
328	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.tautologicalQuotient}
329	  \uses{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf, def:gr_chart_quotient,
330	    def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_cocycle,
331	    def:scheme_modules_glue}
332	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
333	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
334	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L908-L910).
335	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
336	  % "This is compatible with the homomorphisms $u^I$,
337	  %  so we get a surjective homomorphism
338	  %  $u : \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$."
339	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
340	  The chart quotients \(u^I : \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\)
341	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}) are compatible with the bundle gluing
342	  \((g_{I,J})\) of \(\mathcal{U}\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}): over
343	  each overlap \(U^I_J\) one has the identity of \(d \times r\) matrices
344	  \[
345	    g_{I,J}\, X^I \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I \;=\; X^J \qquad \text{on } U^I_J,
346	  \]
347	  which is precisely the defining formula of the chart transition
348	  \(\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}).
349	  Hence the local maps \(u^I\), transported through the trivialisations of
350	  \(\mathcal{U}\), agree on overlaps and glue to a single surjective
351	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}\)-linear homomorphism
352	  \[
353	    u \;:\; \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\,r}
354	      \;\twoheadrightarrow\; \mathcal{U},
355	  \]
356	  the \emph{tautological} (or \emph{universal}) rank-\(d\) quotient of the trivial
357	  rank-\(r\) sheaf. It is surjective because each \(u^I\) is (split) surjective and
358	  surjectivity is local on \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\), and \(\mathcal{U}\) is locally
359	  free of rank \(d\) by \cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}. The pair
360	  \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\) is the family of rank-\(d\) quotients carried
361	  by \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) itself.
362	\end{definition}

364	\section{The functor of points}
365	\label{sec:grquot_functor}
366	
367	\begin{definition}
368	
369	\leanok
370	[The Grassmannian / Quot-of-trivial-sheaf functor \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\)]
371	  \label{def:grassmannian_functor}
372	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.functor}
373	  \uses{def:is_locally_free_of_rank, def:quot_functor}
374	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
375	  % Exercise (2) "Grassmannian as a Quot scheme" (read from
376	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L557-L564).
377	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
378	  % "represents the
379	  %  contravariant functor
380	  %  $$\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$$
381	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any $T$ the
382	  %  set of all equivalence classes
383	  %  $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
384	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$."
385	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2).}
386	  The \emph{Grassmannian functor}
387	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d) = \Quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}
388	  \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/\mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) is the contravariant functor
389	  from schemes to sets defined as follows. For a scheme \(T\), a \emph{family of
390	  rank-\(d\) quotients of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\)} is a pair
391	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) consisting of a locally free
392	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-module \(\mathcal{F}\) of rank \(d\) together with a surjective
393	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-linear homomorphism \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
394	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\). Two such families
395	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) and \(\langle \mathcal{F}', q' \rangle\) are
396	  \emph{equivalent} if there is an isomorphism \(f : \mathcal{F}
397	  \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{F}'\) with \(f \circ q = q'\); equivalently
398	  \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\) as subsheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\). Set
399	  \[
400	    \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T)
401	      \;=\; \bigl\{\, \text{equivalence classes } \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle
402	        \text{ of rank-}d\text{ quotients of } \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \,\bigr\}.
403	  \]
404	  For a morphism \(\varphi : T' \to T\), the map
405	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(\varphi)\) sends \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\)
406	  to the pulled-back quotient \(\langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{F},\, \varphi^{*}q
407	  \rangle\); this is well-defined on equivalence classes because pullback is right
408	  exact and preserves local freeness of rank \(d\). (As \(\mathcal{F}\) is locally
409	  free of rank \(d\), surjectivity is preserved and the support is automatically
410	  proper -- all of \(T'\) -- so this is the special case
411	  \(E = \mathcal{O}^{r}\), \(X = S = \operatorname{base}\) of the general Quot
412	  functor of \cref{def:quot_functor}.)
413	\end{definition}
414	
415	\section{The universal property}
416	\label{sec:grquot_universal}
417	
418	\begin{theorem}
419	
420	\leanok
421	[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) represents the Grassmannian functor]
422	  \label{thm:grassmannian_universal_property}
423	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.represents}
424	  \uses{def:grassmannian_functor, def:tautological_quotient,
425	    def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf, def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_affine_chart,
426	    def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle}
427	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
428	  % Exercise (2) "Grassmannian as a Quot scheme" (read from
429	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L546-L566).
430	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
431	  % "Show that $\grass(r,d)$ together with the quotient
432	  %  $u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$ represents the
433	  %  contravariant functor
434	  %  $$\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$$
435	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any $T$ the
436	  %  set of all equivalence classes
437	  %  $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
438	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$.
439	  %  Therefore, $\quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$
440	  %  exists, and equals $\grass(r,d)$."
441	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2).}
442	  The Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}),
443	  together with the tautological quotient \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\)
444	  (\cref{def:tautological_quotient}), represents the functor
445	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:grassmannian_functor}). That is, for
446	  every scheme \(T\) the natural map
447	  \[
448	    \operatorname{Hom}(T, \mathrm{Gr}(r,d))
449	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
450	    \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T),
451	    \qquad
452	    \bigl(\,\varphi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\,\bigr)
453	      \;\longmapsto\;
454	    \langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U},\, \varphi^{*}u \rangle,
455	  \]
456	  is a bijection, natural in \(T\). Consequently the Quot scheme
457	  \(\quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/
458	  \mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) of rank-\(d\) quotients of the trivial rank-\(r\) sheaf
459	  exists and equals \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\); the tautological quotient is the
460	  universal family.
461	\end{theorem}
462	
463	\begin{proof}
464	  \uses{def:tautological_quotient, def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf,
465	    def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_universal_matrix,
466	    def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle, def:gr_glued_scheme,
467	    def:grassmannian_functor}
468	  Naturality of \(\varphi \mapsto \langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U}, \varphi^{*}u
469	  \rangle\) is immediate: pullback is functorial and \(\varphi^{*}u\) is again a
470	  surjection onto a rank-\(d\) locally free sheaf, so the assignment defines a
471	  morphism of functors \(\operatorname{Hom}(-,\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)) \to
472	  \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\). It remains to show it is a bijection for each
473	  \(T\); we exhibit an inverse, constructed Zariski-locally on \(T\) and glued.
474	
475	  \emph{From a quotient to a morphism.} Let \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle \in
476	  \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T)\), so \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow
477	  \mathcal{F}\) with \(\mathcal{F}\) locally free of rank \(d\). For a size-\(d\)
478	  subset \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) let \(T_I \subseteq T\) be the locus where
479	  the composite
480	  \[
481	    \mathcal{O}_T^{\,d}
482	      \overset{\ \iota_I\ }{\hookrightarrow} \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
483	      \xrightarrow{\ q\ } \mathcal{F},
484	  \]
485	  with \(\iota_I\) the inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates, is an
486	  isomorphism. This locus is open: in a local trivialisation of \(\mathcal{F}\)
487	  the composite is a \(d \times d\) matrix and \(T_I\) is the non-vanishing locus
488	  of its determinant. The \(T_I\) cover \(T\): at any point \(t\), the
489	  \(d\)-dimensional fibre quotient \(q(t) : \kappa(t)^{r} \twoheadrightarrow
490	  \mathcal{F}\otimes\kappa(t)\) is surjective, so some \(d\) of the \(r\) standard
491	  basis vectors map to a basis of the \(d\)-dimensional fibre, i.e.\ \(t \in T_I\)
492	  for that \(I\) (by Nakayama the isomorphism then holds in a neighbourhood).
493	
494	  On \(T_I\), trivialise \(\mathcal{F}\) using \(\iota_I^{*}\): the quotient
495	  becomes a surjection \(q_I : \mathcal{O}_{T_I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{T_I}^{\,d}\)
496	  represented by a \(d \times r\) matrix \(M^I\) whose \(I\)-th minor \(M^I_I\) is
497	  the identity \(1_{d \times d}\) -- exactly the shape of the universal matrix
498	  \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}). The \(d(r-d)\) free entries of \(M^I\)
499	  are global functions on \(T_I\) and hence determine a unique
500	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra homomorphism \(R^I = \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to
501	  \Gamma(T_I, \mathcal{O}_{T_I})\) sending \(x^I_{p,q}\) to the corresponding entry
502	  of \(M^I\), i.e.\ a unique morphism \(\varphi_I : T_I \to U^I\)
503	  (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) with \(\varphi_I^{*}X^I = M^I\) and thus
504	  \(\varphi_I^{*}u^I = q_I\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}).
505	
506	  \emph{Gluing.} On the overlap \(T_I \cap T_J\) the two trivialisations differ by
507	  the change-of-basis \((M^I_J)^{-1}\), which is exactly the bundle transition
508	  \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1}\) of \(\mathcal{U}\)
509	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}); concretely
510	  \(M^J = (M^I_J)^{-1} M^I = \varphi_I^{*}\theta_{I,J}(X^J)\)
511	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}). Hence
512	  \(\varphi_I\) and \(\varphi_J\) land in the overlap charts and agree there after
513	  the transition \(\theta_{I,J}\), so by the cocycle condition
514	  (\cref{lem:gr_cocycle}) the \(\varphi_I\) glue to a single morphism
515	  \(\varphi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}). By
516	  construction \(\varphi^{*}\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle = \langle \mathcal{F},
517	  q \rangle\), since this holds on each \(T_I\) and the equivalence class is
518	  determined by \(\ker(q)\).
519	
520	  \emph{Uniqueness.} If \(\psi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) also satisfies
521	  \(\psi^{*}\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\), then
522	  on \(\psi^{-1}(U^I)\) the pulled-back tautological quotient \(\psi^{*}u^I\)
523	  equals \(q\) in the \(I\)-trivialisation, which forces \(\psi^{*}X^I = M^I\) and
524	  hence \(\psi = \varphi_I\) there; as the \(U^I\) cover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\),
525	  \(\psi = \varphi\). Thus \(\varphi\) is the unique morphism inducing
526	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\), and \(\varphi \mapsto \langle
527	  \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U}, \varphi^{*}u \rangle\) is bijective.
528	
529	  Therefore \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) with \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\) is a fine
530	  moduli space: it represents \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\), so
531	  \(\quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/
532	  \mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) exists and equals \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\).
533	\end{proof}

79	/-- The **chart quotient** `u^I : O_{U^I}^r → O_{U^I}^d` (`def:gr_chart_quotient`):
80	left multiplication by the universal matrix `X^I` (`universalMatrix`). It is realised as
81	the morphism of free sheaves of modules whose matrix of components, in the standard bases
82	`(e_{i'})_{i' : Fin r}` and `(e_p)_{p : Fin d}`, is the universal matrix `X^I` injected
83	into the structure sheaf via `Scheme.ΓSpecIso`. Since the `I`-minor of `X^I` is the
84	identity, `u^I` is a split surjection onto the free rank-`d` sheaf.

90	  let A := CommRingCat.of (MvPolynomial (Fin d × {q : Fin r // q ∉ I}) ℤ)
91	  let R := (affineChart d r I).ringCatSheaf
92	  haveI : HasFiniteBiproducts (SheafOfModules R) :=
93	    HasFiniteBiproducts.of_hasFiniteProducts
94	  let M : ∀ (_ : Fin r) (_ : Fin d), SheafOfModules.unit R ⟶ SheafOfModules.unit R :=
95	    fun i' p => scalarEnd ((Scheme.ΓSpecIso A).inv.hom ((universalMatrix d r I hI) p i'))
96	  (biproduct.isoCoproduct (fun _ : Fin r => SheafOfModules.unit R)).symm.hom ≫
97	    biproduct.matrix M ≫

102	restriction of `u^I` is the identity, the matrix-level content of `lem:gr_chartQuotientMap_epi`. -/
103	private lemma chartQuotientMap_ιFree (d r : ℕ) (I : Finset (Fin r)) (hI : I.card = d)
104	    (k : Fin d) :
105	    SheafOfModules.ιFree (R := (affineChart d r I).ringCatSheaf)
106	        ((I.orderIsoOfFin hI k : Fin r)) ≫ chartQuotientMap d r I hI
107	      = SheafOfModules.ιFree k := by
108	  set A := CommRingCat.of (MvPolynomial (Fin d × {q : Fin r // q ∉ I}) ℤ) with hA
109	  set S := AlgebraicGeometry.Spec A with hS
110	  haveI : HasFiniteBiproducts (SheafOfModules S.ringCatSheaf) :=
111	    HasFiniteBiproducts.of_hasFiniteProducts
112	  change SheafOfModules.ιFree (↑((I.orderIsoOfFin hI) k)) ≫
113	      ((biproduct.isoCoproduct
114	            (fun _ : Fin r => SheafOfModules.unit S.ringCatSheaf)).symm.hom ≫
115	        biproduct.matrix (fun (i' : Fin r) (p : Fin d) => scalarEnd
116	          ((Scheme.ΓSpecIso A).inv.hom (universalMatrix d r I hI p i'))) ≫
117	        (biproduct.isoCoproduct
118	            (fun _ : Fin d => SheafOfModules.unit S.ringCatSheaf)).hom)
119	      = SheafOfModules.ιFree k
120	  rw [Iso.symm_hom, SheafOfModules.ιFree, biproduct.isoCoproduct_inv]
121	  erw [Sigma.ι_desc_assoc]
122	  rw [biproduct.ι_matrix_assoc, biproduct.isoCoproduct_hom]
123	  have h1 : (CommRingCat.Hom.hom (Scheme.ΓSpecIso A).inv) (1 : A) = 1 := map_one _
124	  have h0 : (CommRingCat.Hom.hom (Scheme.ΓSpecIso A).inv) (0 : A) = 0 := map_zero _
125	  have hsub := universalMatrix_submatrix_self d r I hI
126	  have lift_eq :
127	      (biproduct.lift fun p : Fin d => scalarEnd
128	          ((Scheme.ΓSpecIso A).inv.hom (universalMatrix d r I hI p (↑((I.orderIsoOfFin hI) k)))))
129	        = biproduct.ι (fun _ : Fin d => SheafOfModules.unit S.ringCatSheaf) k := by
130	    refine biproduct.hom_ext _ _ (fun p => ?_)
131	    rw [biproduct.lift_π]
132	    have hentry : universalMatrix d r I hI p (↑((I.orderIsoOfFin hI) k))
133	        = (1 : Matrix (Fin d) (Fin d) A) p k :=
134	      congrFun (congrFun hsub p) k
135	    rw [hentry, Matrix.one_apply]
136	    by_cases hpk : p = k
137	    · rw [if_pos hpk, h1, scalarEnd_one, hpk, biproduct.ι_π_self]
138	    · rw [if_neg hpk, h0, scalarEnd_zero, biproduct.ι_π_ne _ (Ne.symm hpk)]
139	  rw [lift_eq, biproduct.ι_desc]

151	    have key : SheafOfModules.ιFree (R := (affineChart d r I).ringCatSheaf) k ≫

154	        = SheafOfModules.ιFree k :=

162	/-! ## Gluing a sheaf of modules along a scheme glue datum
163	
164	`Scheme.Modules.glue` descends a sheaf of modules from per-chart data plus a transition
165	cocycle over a `Scheme.GlueData`. Mathlib carries no turn-key module descent over a
166	scheme glue datum (confirmed), so this is an Archon-original construction. The
167	construction path (blueprint `def:scheme_modules_glue`): restrict to a chart through the
168	open-immersion pullback equivalence (`Scheme.Modules.overRestrictPullbackIso`) and glue
169	the local sections by locality of sections (`existsUnique_gluing'`).

171	NOTE (scaffold): the body and the module-cocycle hypotheses on `g` are still to be filled;
172	the transition data `g` (per-overlap pullback isos) is recorded in the signature, the
173	multiplicative cocycle conditions remain to be added before the construction is closed. -/

177	/-- **Gluing a sheaf of modules along an open cover given by a scheme glue datum**
178	(`def:scheme_modules_glue`). From a glue datum `D`, per-chart sheaves of modules `M i`,
179	and transition isomorphisms `g i j` comparing the two charts' sheaves over the overlap
180	`V (i,j)` (after pullback), produces a glued sheaf of `O_{D.glued}`-modules.
181	
182	Project-local: Mathlib has no module descent over a scheme glue datum. -/
183	noncomputable def glue (D : Scheme.GlueData)
184	    (M : ∀ i, (D.U i).Modules)
185	    (g : ∀ i j, (Scheme.Modules.pullback (D.f i j)).obj (M i) ≅
186	        (Scheme.Modules.pullback (D.t i j ≫ D.f j i)).obj (M j))
187	    -- (C1) self-identity: over the diagonal overlap `V (i,i)` (where `t i i = 𝟙`) the
188	    -- transition isomorphism is the identity, i.e. the canonical isomorphism induced by
189	    -- `f i i = t i i ≫ f i i` (blueprint `def:scheme_modules_glue` (C1)).
190	    (_hC1 : ∀ i, g i i = eqToIso (congrArg (fun φ => (Scheme.Modules.pullback φ).obj (M i))
191	        (show D.f i i = D.t i i ≫ D.f i i by rw [D.t_id i, Category.id_comp]))) :
192	    D.glued.Modules :=
193	  sorry
194	
195	end AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules

requires a small piece of base-change machinery for the pullback functor on sheaves
of modules: the three transition isomorphisms attached to a triple of charts live a
priori on three different overlaps, and to compare their composite they must be
transported to a single common overlap. The next two blocks supply exactly this
transport -- the pseudofunctorial comparison of iterated pullbacks
(\cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}) and its packaging along the triple-overlap data of a
scheme glue datum (\cref{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport}) -- after which the
cocycle condition can be expressed cleanly.

\begin{definition}[Comparison of iterated pullbacks of sheaves of modules]
  \label{def:modules_pullbackComp}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp}
  \mathlibok
  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
  Let \(a : X \to Y\) and \(b : Y \to Z\) be morphisms of schemes. For a sheaf of
  \(\mathcal{O}_Z\)-modules \(\mathcal{M}\), pulling back first along \(b\) and then
  along \(a\) agrees with pulling back along the composite \(b \circ a\): there is a
  natural isomorphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
  \[
    a^{*}\bigl(b^{*}\mathcal{M}\bigr)
      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
    (b \circ a)^{*}\mathcal{M},
  \]
  natural in \(\mathcal{M}\), expressing that \(\mathcal{M} \mapsto \mathcal{M}\)'s
  pullback is a pseudofunctor on schemes. These comparison isomorphisms satisfy the
  pentagon (associativity) coherence for a triple of composable morphisms. This is the
  pullback half of the pseudofunctoriality of sheaves of modules and is supplied by
  Mathlib.
\end{definition}

\begin{lemma}[Base-change transport of a transition isomorphism to a triple overlap]
  \label{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport}
  \uses{def:modules_pullbackComp, def:gr_the_glue_data}
  Let \(D\) be a glue datum of schemes (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}) with charts
  \(U_i\), overlaps \(V_{ij}\), overlap immersions \(f_{ij} : V_{ij} \hookrightarrow
  U_i\), transitions \(t_{ij} : V_{ij} \xrightarrow{\sim} V_{ji}\), and triple-overlap
  data \(t'_{ijk}\) with its compatibility identity \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\). For an index
  triple \((i,j,k)\) write
  \[
    V_{ijk} \;=\; V_{ij} \times_{U_i} V_{ik}
  \]
  for the common triple overlap, with the two projections
  \(p^{ij}_{ijk} : V_{ijk} \to V_{ij}\) and \(p^{ik}_{ijk} : V_{ijk} \to V_{ik}\), and
  let \(t'_{ijk} : V_{ijk} \xrightarrow{\sim} V_{jki}\) be the glue datum's triple
  transition, satisfying
  \[
    t'_{ijk} \,\circ\, p^{jk}_{jki} \;=\; p^{ij}_{ijk} \,\circ\, t_{ij}
    \qquad (t_{\mathrm{fac}}).
  \]
  Given per-chart sheaves of modules \(\mathcal{M}_i\) and a transition isomorphism
  \[
    g_{ij} \;:\; f_{ij}^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_i
      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
    (t_{ij} \circ f_{ji})^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_j
  \]
  on the overlap \(V_{ij}\) (as in \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue}), the
  \emph{base-change transport} of \(g_{ij}\) to the triple overlap \(V_{ijk}\) is the
  isomorphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_{V_{ijk}}\)-modules
  \[
    \widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}
      \;:\;
    (f_{ij} \circ p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_i
      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
    (t_{ij} \circ f_{ji} \circ p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_j
  \]
  obtained by pulling \(g_{ij}\) back along \(p^{ij}_{ijk}\) and reassociating the
  iterated pullbacks via \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}. With the analogous transports
  \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i}\) and \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\) (the first taken along
  \(t'_{ijk}\), so that \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\) identifies its source and target sheaves
  with those of \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\) and \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\)), all three
  transported isomorphisms become endomorphism-composable: \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\)
  and \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i}\) land on a common sheaf, and their composite is
  comparable to \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\) on \(V_{ijk}\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:modules_pullbackComp, def:gr_the_glue_data}
  The pullback functor on sheaves of modules is a pseudofunctor: for composable
  scheme morphisms it carries the comparison isomorphisms of
  \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}, natural in the sheaf and coherent for triple
  composites. Pulling the transition isomorphism \(g_{ij}\) back along the projection
  \(p^{ij}_{ijk} : V_{ijk} \to V_{ij}\) yields an isomorphism between
  \((p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*} f_{ij}^{*}\mathcal{M}_i\) and \((p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}(t_{ij} \circ
  f_{ji})^{*}\mathcal{M}_j\); applying \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp} to each side
  reassociates these into the single-pullback form
  \((f_{ij} \circ p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}\mathcal{M}_i\) and
  \((t_{ij} \circ f_{ji} \circ p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}\mathcal{M}_j\), giving
  \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\). For the \((j,k)\)-transition the same construction is
  applied along the triple transition \(t'_{ijk}\) rather than a bare projection; the
  glue datum's compatibility identity \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\),
  \(t'_{ijk} \circ p^{jk}_{jki} = p^{ij}_{ijk} \circ t_{ij}\), guarantees that the
  source of \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i}\) coincides with the target of
  \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\) (both are the pullback of \(\mathcal{M}_j\) along the same
  morphism \(V_{ijk} \to U_j\)), so the two are composable as endomorphism-style
  isomorphisms over \(V_{ijk}\). The reassociations introduced by
  \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp} are coherent (they satisfy the pentagon identity), so
  the composite \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i} \circ \widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\) and the third
  transport \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\) are isomorphisms between the same pair of
  sheaves on \(V_{ijk}\), and may be compared directly. This is the module-level
  analogue of the scheme-level triangle \(t'_{ijk}\)/\(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\) and is the
  shape in which the multiplicative cocycle condition is stated below.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[The $I$-columns of the chart quotient are the identity]
  \label{lem:gr_chartQuotientMap_iFree}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartQuotientMap_ιFree}
  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_scalarEnd,
    lem:gr_scalarEnd_one, lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero,
    lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
  For each \(k \in \{1,\dots,d\}\), the chart quotient \(u^I\)
  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}) carries the \(\iota_I(k)\)-th basis section of the
  free sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\) -- where \(\iota_I(k)\) is the \(k\)-th
  element of \(I\) in increasing order -- to the \(k\)-th basis section of
  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\); that is, the \(I\)-indexed columns of \(u^I\) form the
  identity:
  \[
    e_{\iota_I(k)} \,\circ\, u^I \;=\; e_k.
  \]
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_scalarEnd,
    lem:gr_scalarEnd_one, lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero,
    lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
  By the biproduct presentation of \(u^I\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}), the
  composite \(e_{\iota_I(k)} \circ u^I\) reads off the \(\iota_I(k)\)-th column of the
  component matrix, whose \(p\)-th entry is the scalar endomorphism
  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(X^I_{p,\iota_I(k)})\). The \(I\)-indexed columns of the
  universal matrix \(X^I\), read through the order isomorphism \(\{1,\dots,d\} \cong
  I\), form the \(d \times d\) identity (\cref{lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}),
  so \(X^I_{p,\iota_I(k)} = \delta_{p,k}\). Hence the diagonal entry is
  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(1) = \mathrm{id}\) (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_one}) and the
  off-diagonal entries are \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(0) = 0\)
  (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero}); the resulting column is the \(k\)-th coordinate
  inclusion, giving \(e_{\iota_I(k)} \circ u^I = e_k\).
\end{proof}
